findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is gross cardiomegaly with an increased number and size of well-defined pulmonary vessels, indicating shunt vascularity. There is global cardiac enlargement involving all four chambers, particularly the right atrium and both ventricles.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include a normal-sized aorta and potential skeletal anomalies such as 11 pairs of ribs or multiple manubrial ossification centers, which are strongly associated with Trisomy 21. Cross-sectional imaging may demonstrate the classic 'gooseneck' sign due to shortening of the left ventricular inflow tract and elongation of the left ventricular outflow tract.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of cyanotic congenital heart disease features such as decreased pulmonary vascularity or a boot-shaped heart. Furthermore, the aortic arch is not enlarged, which helps differentiate this from a patent ductus arteriosus.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the combination of shunt vascularity, global cardiomegaly involving all four chambers including the right atrium, and a normal-sized aorta.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Atrioventricular canal defect, given the global cardiomegaly and shunt vascularity without aortic enlargement. This defect, also known as an endocardial cushion defect, occurs due to inadequate fusion of the atrial and ventricular septa and is highly associated with Down syndrome.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Ventricular septal defect (VSD), however isolated VSDs typically present with enlargement of the main pulmonary artery, left atrium, and left ventricle, but characteristically spare the right atrium. In contrast, an atrioventricular canal defect typically enlarges all four chambers, notably including right atrial enlargement.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Atrial septal defect (ASD), given that it also causes shunt vascularity and right-sided chamber enlargement, and missing a large defect could lead to irreversible Eisenmenger syndrome. However, ASD typically spares the left ventricle and often presents in an older age group compared to the early clinical presentation of an AV canal defect.
differentials.unifyingSummary: Taken together, these findings point to a unifying diagnosis of an Atrioventricular canal defect in the setting of Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome).

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately evaluate the extent of the septal defects, assess the morphology of the atrioventricular valves, and quantify the degree of left-to-right shunting.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison with fetal echocardiography or antenatal ultrasound is useful to determine if the diagnosis was suspected prior to birth and to establish a baseline for cardiac chamber sizes.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated postnatal echocardiogram as the first-line investigation, followed by cardiac MRI if further anatomical detailing is required, as this modality is superior here for quantifying the shunt fraction (Qp:Qs) using phase-contrast sequences and evaluating ventricular function without ionizing radiation.
management.mriSequences: I would specify velocity-encoded phase-contrast sequences to assess shunt volume and steady-state free precession (SSFP) cine sequences to evaluate ventricular function, chamber volumes, and atrioventricular valve regurgitation.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Paediatric Cardiology and Cardiothoracic Surgery MDT. My report would clearly document the size of the septal defects, the degree of chamber enlargement, and any evidence of pulmonary arterial hypertension to guide the timing of surgical repair.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is gross cardiomegaly with an increased number and size of well-defined pulmonary vessels, indicating shunt vascularity. There is global cardiac enlargement involving all four chambers, particularly the right atrium and both ventricles. Supporting features include a normal-sized aorta and potential skeletal anomalies such as 11 pairs of ribs or multiple manubrial ossification centers, which are strongly associated with Trisomy 21. Cross-sectional imaging may demonstrate the classic 'gooseneck' sign due to shortening of the left ventricular inflow tract and elongation of the left ventricular outflow tract. Importantly, there is no evidence of cyanotic congenital heart disease features such as decreased pulmonary vascularity or a boot-shaped heart. Furthermore, the aortic arch is not enlarged, which helps differentiate this from a patent ductus arteriosus. The feature that most influences my thinking is the combination of shunt vascularity, global cardiomegaly involving all four chambers including the right atrium, and a normal-sized aorta.

The appearances are most in keeping with Atrioventricular canal defect, given the global cardiomegaly and shunt vascularity without aortic enlargement. This defect, also known as an endocardial cushion defect, occurs due to inadequate fusion of the atrial and ventricular septa and is highly associated with Down syndrome. The principle differential would be Ventricular septal defect (VSD), however isolated VSDs typically present with enlargement of the main pulmonary artery, left atrium, and left ventricle, but characteristically spare the right atrium. In contrast, an atrioventricular canal defect typically enlarges all four chambers, notably including right atrial enlargement. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Atrial septal defect (ASD), given that it also causes shunt vascularity and right-sided chamber enlargement, and missing a large defect could lead to irreversible Eisenmenger syndrome. However, ASD typically spares the left ventricle and often presents in an older age group compared to the early clinical presentation of an AV canal defect. Taken together, these findings point to a unifying diagnosis of an Atrioventricular canal defect in the setting of Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome).

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately evaluate the extent of the septal defects, assess the morphology of the atrioventricular valves, and quantify the degree of left-to-right shunting. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison with fetal echocardiography or antenatal ultrasound is useful to determine if the diagnosis was suspected prior to birth and to establish a baseline for cardiac chamber sizes. I would recommend a dedicated postnatal echocardiogram as the first-line investigation, followed by cardiac MRI if further anatomical detailing is required, as this modality is superior here for quantifying the shunt fraction (Qp:Qs) using phase-contrast sequences and evaluating ventricular function without ionizing radiation. I would specify velocity-encoded phase-contrast sequences to assess shunt volume and steady-state free precession (SSFP) cine sequences to evaluate ventricular function, chamber volumes, and atrioventricular valve regurgitation. This should go to the Paediatric Cardiology and Cardiothoracic Surgery MDT. My report would clearly document the size of the septal defects, the degree of chamber enlargement, and any evidence of pulmonary arterial hypertension to guide the timing of surgical repair.
